visits.text: The big day: swimming with dolphins. Wake 5-5:30am, departure from Albion at 6am, boarding at La Jetty Road in Black River at 6:45am. On the west coast, two species - the spinner dolphin, in large playful groups, and the bottlenose dolphin, more discreet. Boats cut the engine on approach, you enter the water gently. Favor Vitamin Sea or Dolswim: 8 people max, ethical approach. Avoid: boats that chase and encircle, it's become a real problem here. Count on 2,500 MUR/adult, 1,800 MUR/child with serious operators.

La Preneuse beach for recovery after the dolphins: white sand, calm lagoon, perfect for recovering from the 5am wake. The coral reef is 50 meters out. The casuarinas give shade all day, food trucks on weekends. Kids can sleep on the beach while parents read.

La Preneuse Martello Tower, British fortification from 1810 built to defend the Black River mouth against the French. Small interior museum (cannons, uniforms, battle plans), entrance 200 MUR, visit in 45 minutes. Panoramic view from the roof. Ideal for history-curious teenagers - big chunk of explanation on the Napoleonic Wars seen from the Indian Ocean.

Humpback whale outing departing from Black River. July is in the heart of the season (June to October): females come to give birth in Mauritian warm waters, barely 1 km from the west coast. Spectacle guaranteed 90% in this period - full breaches, tail slaps. Warning: swimming with whales is not authorized, boat observation only (at regulatory 100m). Longer outing than dolphins, 4-5h. Vitamin Sea offers a combo dolphins + whales + sperm whales over 5h, at 4,000 MUR/adult.

Casela Nature Park as an afternoon option (or full day if you skip whales), 10 minutes from Albion. 350 hectares, mix between animal park (giraffes to feed, zebras, ostriches) and adventure park. For the 6-year-old: Pangia Kids Park (mini-farm, carousel, mini-golf). For the teen: 400m zip-line, the longest in the Indian Ocean, or the 40m canyon swing. Reserved for 14+ with parents for these latter. On the feline side, the park offers 'walking with lions' and tigers in drive-thru - questionable animal welfare practices, avoid.

Black River Gorges National Park, 6,754 hectares created in 1994, 2% of Mauritian territory. For a family with a 6-year-old, two simple options: the Macchabée Forest Walk (2.5 km flat in shade, 1h30, departure from Pétrin center) or the combination Gorges Viewpoint by car + short trail (15 min walk, spectacular view over the valley). Avoid the climb to Piton de la Petite Rivière Noire (828m, highest point) with a young child, it's too much. 9 endemic bird species observable: Mauritius fody, pink pigeon, Mauritius kestrel - patience and binoculars.

Grand Bassin (Ganga Talao), 3 minutes from Pétrin, crater lake sacred to the Mauritian Hindu community (52% of the population). 33-meter Shiva statue erected on the lake shore, one of the tallest in the world. Massive pilgrimage late February-early March (Maha Shivaratri, up to 500,000 faithful). In July, calm and majestic atmosphere. Covering clothing requested (shoulders and knees).

Chamarel and the Seven-Colored Earth, 30 minutes out of the park. Unique geological phenomenon: volcanic ash dunes in 7 shades (red, brown, purple, green, blue, crimson, yellow). Small, but clearly worth seeing once. On the same site, view of Chamarel waterfall (100m drop) and an Aldabra tortoise park. Combined ticket 350 MUR/adult, free under 3.

Domaine de Bel Ombre, 2,500 hectares preserved on the wild south coast. Far from the crowds, this is as close as it gets to pre-tourism Mauritius. Activities: horseback nature ride (from 6 years double with guide), guided hike to Alexandra waterfall (50m), 2-seater buggy on estate tracks (1h30, 3,800 MUR per buggy). La Citronnelle restaurant serves the best rum-grilled prawns on the island.

Flic en Flac beach 15 minutes north of Albion, 8 km of white sand, one of the island's largest. Ideal lagoon for children: shallow for 200 meters, sandy bottom. Casuarinas for shade. The southern portion (Wolmar) is wilder, the northern portion more lively. Snack at Domino's Pizza or Chez Pepe for cheap lunch.

activities.text: Nap on Albion beach after the dawn wake-up. The public beach is small but deserted on weekdays, direct access from the village. Albion lighthouse visible in the distance (the only one on the island, built in 1910).

Creole dinner at Pavillon de Jade or Domaine Anna in Flic en Flac: grilled fish (captain or red snapper), sausage rougaille, homemade dessert. Mauritians also go there on Friday nights, sign that it's good. Count on 600-800 MUR per adult main course.

Buggy or electric eco-rider at Casela through the Yemen reserve (4,500 hectares, one of the largest private wild spaces on the island). Javan deer in herds (about 9,000 individuals), wild boars, mongooses. 2h ride, departures 9:15am and 2:45pm. Minimum age 16 to drive a buggy, so dad/mom at the wheel and passengers in back. The silent 4x4 eco-ride is open to the whole family (from 4 years).

Sunset at Tamarin on the surfers' beach, 5 minutes south of Albion. Mauritius's best-known waves are here: 1m to 2m in July, intermediate level. Guaranteed spectacle, surf school possible for the teen (1,500 MUR for 1h30 session). Le Dock beach bar 50m away, mocktails and tapas.

Tasting break at Rhumerie de Chamarel for adults (distillery tour 1h, tasting of 5 agricultural rums at 350 MUR/person). L'Alchimiste restaurant on site, revisited Creole cuisine, colonial veranda atmosphere. Meal 1,200 MUR/adult, children's menu 450 MUR. Kids receive freshly pressed sugarcane juice during the tasting.

Picnic at Pétrin Information Centre before the walk, or buy chili cakes at Plaine Champagne food trucks. These street vendors are the island's best for local fried snacks - locals stop here coming down from pilgrimages.

Discovery dive at Flic en Flac for the teen (from 8 years with PADI certified instructor). Several centers: Sun Divers, Exploration Sous-Marine. Baptism in pool then sea outing in the 5m range, view of the coral reef. Count on 2,800 MUR for baptism with photos. The 6-year-old can snorkel during this time.

Farewell dinner at La Pirogue or at Le Bistrot du Boucanier in Flic en Flac, last night before the all-inclusive at Le Morne. Creole-French fusion cuisine, terrace facing the sunset. Count on 1,500 MUR/adult. Good address for Mauritian seafood (prawns, lobsters, crabs).

days_plan.itin_title: Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne

places.name: Blue Bay

visits.text: First encounter with the island: Blue Bay beach and its marine park, protected since 2004. This is Mauritius's only lagoon with Marine Park status, with electric-blue water that gives the place its name. The coral reef starts practically at swimming distance from the public beach: pack fins, mask and snorkel and the kids can watch parrotfish and surgeonfish in 1 meter of water. Perfect for a first afternoon without driving too far from the airport. Avoid the area on Saturday-Sunday when it's packed with Mauritian family picnics - charming but noisy.

The Mahébourg waterfront for a gentle arrival. Founded in 1804 under Decaen as a former capital, the town has retained a provincial character that contrasts with Port Louis. Stroll along the waterfront, vendors selling chili cakes and samosas for 5 rupees each. Kids quickly connect with locals playing dominoes on the benches. If you arrive on a Monday morning, the big colorful bazaar is worth the detour - otherwise Saturday's market is even better stocked.

Île aux Aigrettes, 850 meters offshore from Mahébourg: probably the most striking nature visit on the island for children. 26 hectares of coral limestone (so not volcanic like the rest of Mauritius) managed since 1986 by the Mauritius Wildlife Foundation. You walk among giant Aldabra tortoises weighing over 200 kg, some centenarians, reintroduced to replace the Mauritian tortoises extinct around 1735. Guided tour mandatory, 1h30 on the Dodo Trail. Book 2-3 days ahead at 631 2396, the 9:30am slots fill up fast. Price: around 1000 MUR adult, 500 MUR children 4-11 years.

The Mahébourg National History Museum, housed in the Château Gheude, an 18th-century French colonial mansion. Room dedicated to the naval Battle of Grand Port (August 1810), the only French naval victory inscribed on the Arc de Triomphe in Paris. Teenagers often engage thanks to the ship models and cannons. Small but well-done museum, free, perfect mid-day if the heat's beating down. Closed Tuesdays.

The Dutch ruins of Vieux Grand Port, 10 minutes north of Mahébourg. This is where the Dutch first set foot in 1598 - the birth certificate of the island's modern history. Modest ruins (foundations, tombs), but the panorama over the bay is superb and the site is deserted. A chance to explain to the kids that the dodo, exterminated here in less than a century, lived its final days in these forests.

La Vanille Park in Rivière des Anguilles, the almost universal favorite for families with young children. 1,000 giant Aldabra tortoises in semi-free roaming, 2,000 Nile crocodiles bred on site, and an insectarium that gives teenagers the shivers. The big show: crocodile feeding, daily except Monday and Friday at 11:30am. Children can climb on baby tortoises' backs in the educational enclosure. Allow 2 to 3 hours on site. Decent restaurant on site if you want to continue.

Rochester Falls at Souillac, an unusual waterfall formed by rectangular basalt prisms sculpted by erosion. Modest height, 10 meters, but the geological effect is unique on the island. Access via a rutted track through sugarcane fields - better to have a high-clearance vehicle. Swimming possible in the pool, provided rains haven't fallen in recent days (current). Avoid after 4pm, the surroundings empty out quickly.

Gris-Gris, cliff battered by the wild southern swells. No coral reef here, so the ocean hits the rock directly: waves explode 10 meters high on the 'Weeping Rock', a limestone formation carved by salt. This is the only place in Mauritius that resembles the Irish coast, which always surprises. Swimming strictly forbidden, violent currents. Five minutes' walk from parking, accessible to young children.

activities.text: Peaceful swimming at Pointe d'Esny, right next to Blue Bay but far less crowded. White sand beach, shallow lagoon extending 200 meters offshore - ideal for the 6-year-old who can splash around without worry. A few casuarina trees provide shade in the afternoon. No street vendors here, you feel more at home than on a postcard beach.

Glass-bottom boat in Blue Bay Marine Park. 30 minutes above the coral gardens: table coral, brain coral, anemones, and with luck some green turtles. Perfect format for the 6-year-old who won't snorkel for long. Count on 800 MUR per adult, half-price for children. The boats leave from Blue Bay public beach, you can negotiate on site without trouble.

Mahébourg market on Saturday morning, or Curepipe central market as an alternative. All the fruits of the austral winter are there: incredibly sweet lychees, José mangoes, Victoria pineapples. Taste the chili-tamarind fruit salad, strange but addictive. The kids choose their fruits, vendors often offer a piece to taste - it's the custom.

Visit to Saint Aubin at La Flora to wrap up the southern day. 1819 colonial house, former sugar mill converted: they still produce agricultural rum and vanilla. Guided distillery tour (1h), tasting for adults, freshly pressed sugarcane juice for kids. The veranda restaurant serves authentic Creole cuisine - the octopus vindaye is worth it if you're not put off by octopus.

Snack in Souillac: the famous chili cakes and samosas at Gardénia, a local institution since the 1970s. It's touristy, but well done - and far more instructive about Mauritian street food than hotel demonstrations. Count on 80 MUR for a snack for four.

days_plan.itin_title: Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne

map_keywords: La Vanille Nature Park Rivière des Anguilles

places.name: Cap Malheureux

visits.text: Île d'Ambre is reached by kayak from Rivière La Chaux, at the northeast tip. Allow 2 to 3 hours for an excursion through the mangrove, with native forest and ruins of an old colonial quarantine station on the island. Operators offer these guided trips. No postcard beach – raw, little-visited nature.

Roches Noires: Mauritius's northeast coast is the island's least touristy. Black basalt, rough sea, constant wind. Poste Lafayette is a wild beach with wind and few people. Not a calm swimming beach – a coast for watching the sea.

Notre-Dame Auxiliatrice Church at Cap Malheureux has a bright red roof that stands out against the sky and sea – it's the most photographed image of Mauritius. Across the water, on clear days, you see Coin de Mire distinctly. A five-minute stop is enough, but evening light is best.

activities.text: The northeast coast is raw Mauritius – no resort, no polished lagoon. A good half-day's drive on small coastal roads between fishing villages. Plan to buy fresh fish at one of the roadside markets late in the day.

days_plan.itin_title: Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast

map_keywords: Île d'Ambre kayak La Chaux River

visits.text: Grand Bassin – Ganga Talao in Creole – is a crater lake at 550 meters altitude surrounded by multicolored Hindu temples. A 33-meter statue of Shiva greets you at the entrance. It's the most important pilgrimage site in Mauritius for the Hindu community. In February-March, during Maha Shivaratri, hundreds of thousands of pilgrims walk barefoot from across the island – sometimes 40 kilometers on foot to reach the lake.

Black River Gorges National Park covers 6,574 hectares – it's the largest area of native forest still standing in Mauritius. The Macchabée trails cut through canopy forest with tree ferns and rare endemic fauna. Allow 3 to 5 hours depending on your pace. Pack water, closed-toe shoes, and sunscreen.

Plaine Champagne is a 700-meter-high plateau with moorland landscapes unlike anything else in Mauritius. Alexandra Falls plunges into the gorges from this plateau – the viewpoint over the falls and the forest below is worth the 20-minute stop.

Arrive at the Seven Coloured Earth right at opening at 8:30am. After 10am, tour buses roll in and it's over. These volcanic earth dunes owe their colors to basalt decomposing into clays: iron oxides give the red and brown, aluminum oxides the blue and purple. The phenomenon is real – the colors never mix, even after torrential rain.

Chamarel Falls drops nearly 100 meters – it's the island's highest waterfall. The viewpoint is accessible from the Seven Coloured Earth site. It's more impressive during the rainy season (December-March), but visible year-round.

Chamarel Rum Distillery is the only arabica coffee operation in Mauritius, grown on volcanic soil at 300 meters altitude. It also produces rum – white rum, aged rum, spiced rum. Plan for the tasting: it's included in the tour and the bottles aren't available everywhere.

activities.text: Physical day – arrive in Chamarel late afternoon and settle in. The village is quiet, accommodations often tucked deep in the greenery. Good evening to do nothing at all.

Ebony Forest is a private reserve of native ebony forest with 140 plant species. The trail climbs to Point Sublime – a plunging view over Le Morne and the entire west coast. Allow 1.5 hours round trip. It's one of the rare chances to see what Mauritius looked like before deforestation.

days_plan.itin_title: Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast

visits.text: Eureka has 109 doors – yes, 109. This 1830 Creole house in Moka is the largest on the island, built in the typical colonial Mascarene style: verandas on three sides, camphor wood, period furniture. Wander through the rooms, look at the yellowed photographs, then head down to the garden – it follows a river where you can swim.

Trou aux Cerfs is a volcanic crater 300 meters across and 85 meters deep right in the heart of Curepipe. A path circles it in less than a kilometer. On clear days, you get the central plateau mountain chain on one side – and sometimes both coasts of the island.

Curepipe is Mauritius's wettest town, perched at 550 meters altitude. Misty in the morning, cool (18-22°C), it has the atmosphere of a colonial mountain town – wooden houses, covered market, slow rhythm. Pack a light sweater for the evening.

Domaine des Aubineaux is a late-19th-century colonial house with Italian frescoes painted on the ceilings and intact period furniture. In the garden, a colophane tree is over 500 years old – one of the oldest trees on the island. Admission is often included in Tea Route combo tickets.

Bois Chéri has been producing tea since 1892 – it's the island's first and oldest plantation. Tour the factory to understand the transformation process from leaf to cup. Then head up to the panoramic restaurant: the view over the south coast on clear days is worth the trip alone. Try the vanilla tea and coconut tea – they're the house signatures. ⚠️ The factory only runs tours certain days of the week: check before you go.

Saint Aubin Estate: a sugar plantation since 1819, one of Mauritius's oldest. It's here that the island's first agricultural rum is produced. The Vanilla House walks you through the entire transformation chain – from the Vanilla planifolia orchid to the dried pod. Allow 1.5 hours to tour the estate.

activities.text: If you're there on a Thursday or Sunday, detour to the Quatre Bornes market – the island's big textile market. Hundreds of stalls selling clothes, sheets, shoes at rock-bottom prices. Not touristy at all: this is where Mauritians do their shopping.

This is a day for the palate. Flavored tea tastings at Bois Chéri, aged rum and cane punch at Saint Aubin, fresh vanilla scraped on the spot. Leave space in your luggage – the teas and rums from these estates aren't as easy to find elsewhere.

days_plan.itin_title: Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast

places.name: Grand Baie

visits.text: Trou aux Biches is the perfect choice for an easy start. The lagoon is protected by a coral reef - the water is flat, crystal-clear, and you can stand far from shore. Arrive mid-morning when the sunlight hits the white sand just right.

Mont Choisy is the longest beach in the north: nearly 2 km lined with shade-giving casuarina trees. Less crowded than Trou aux Biches on weekdays - a solid option if you're looking for some breathing room.

Cap Malheureux owes its name to the shipwrecks that littered this wind-battered headland. The Notre-Dame Auxiliatrice church - red roof, white walls - is one of the most photographed in Mauritius. Facing it, Coin de Mire rises on the horizon about 18 km offshore.

Pereybere is a pocket beach with a different vibe from Grand Baie - more local, less touristy. Vendors selling gâteaux piments walk along the waterfront late morning. Make the walk from Cap Malheureux on foot; it's 10 minutes. Bain Boeuf is 5 minutes further along the coast - a quiet cove, almost tourist-free.

The Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam Botanical Garden in Pamplemousses is one of the oldest in the tropics - founded in 1770 under Pierre Poivre, administrator of the Îles de France. It's home to over 650 species including giant Victoria amazonica water lilies and talipot palms, which flower only once in 60 years before dying. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

L'Aventure du Sucre in Beau Plan is a museum housed in a 19th-century sugar factory. It tells the story of sugarcane - and of the slavery that made it possible. The period machinery is still in place. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours.

Château de Labourdonnais in Mapou is a colonial mansion built in 1859 - one of the few in the country still habitable. The estate includes an orchard with 70 fruit species and a craft rum distillery. The guided tour lasts about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Snorkeling straight from the beach at Trou aux Biches - sea turtles regularly frequent this area. No boat needed. Bring your mask and snorkel if you have them; local operators rent gear on-site.

Sunset from La Cuvette, the small cove at Grand Baie. This is where locals gather at the end of the day - the vibe is authentic, the orientation due west. It's about 15 minutes on foot from Grand Baie town centre.

Option A - if you haven't done the catamaran yet: afternoon swim at Pereybère or Bain Boeuf, local atmosphere, no resort in sight. Option B - if you prefer to dedicate this entire day to the islets: catamaran excursion to Coin de Mire, Île Plate, and Îlot Gabriel (about 6 hours, lunch on board, snorkeling around Îlot Gabriel). If going this route, book the day before and depart in the morning directly from Grand Baie harbour - Cap Malheureux and Pereybère can be done in 1 hour the evening before.
Tasting of rum and artisanal jams at Château de Labourdonnais. The house-made flavoured rums use fruit from the orchard - it's a short but pleasant stop at the end of the tour.

days_plan.itin_title: Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast

map_keywords: Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam Botanical Garden Pamplemousses
L'Aventure du Sucre Beau Plan Mauritius

places.name: Le Morne Brabant

visits.text: The Seven Coloured Earths of Chamarel are a geological curiosity formed by the decomposition of volcanic basalt into clays of different colours - ochre, brick red, violet, khaki green. The dunes are about 100 metres in diameter. What's remarkable is that the colours don't mix even in the rain. The visit takes 30 minutes.

Chamarel Falls plunge about a hundred metres into the gorge below. They're among the highest in Mauritius. The viewing platform is accessible from the Seven Coloured Earths entrance - both visits are done together.

The Rhumerie de Chamarel produces the island's only agricultural rums - and the only arabica coffee grown in Mauritius. Visit the plantations, follow the distillation process, finish with a tasting. Allow 1 hour. If Mauritian coffee intrigues you, this is the only place.

Ebony Forest is a private reserve protecting a forest of endemic black ebony - a critically endangered species. The trail to Point Sublime (about 1 km return) opens onto a 180° view of Le Morne and the west coast. Leave early morning for the best light.

Le Morne Brabant rises to 556 metres and has been UNESCO-listed since 2008 - not for its geology, but for what it represents: in the 18th and 19th centuries, fugitive slaves took refuge in its inaccessible caves. Legend has it that they threw themselves off the cliffs on the day of the abolition of slavery in 1835, believing the soldiers who came to announce it were there to arrest them. The guided ascent takes 3 to 4 hours return. A guide is mandatory to reach the summit - book the day before.

activities.text: Descent to Le Morne mid-afternoon - 15 minutes' drive from Chamarel. The beach at Le Morne opens onto a perfectly calm lagoon on the north side. Swimming, hammock, or simply watching Le Morne Brabant from the water.

Kitesurfing in the Le Morne lagoon - it's one of the world's most renowned spots. The southeast trade wind blows steadily from May to November, and the lagoon is shallow with no current. Operators offer beginner courses. If you don't kitesurf, watching the riders from the beach is already worth something.
Sunset facing Île aux Bénitiers from Le Morne beach - the islet silhouettes to the west, the sky turns orange then red. This is the last proper evening of the circuit. Take it in.

days_plan.itin_title: Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne

map_keywords: Terre des Sept Couleurs Chamarel Mauritius

visits.text: At Gris-Gris, forget the lagoon. Here the ocean pounds straight onto the rock – no coral barrier to slow it down. Wind blows, waves explode on the black cliffs. You'd think you were in Brittany, not the tropics. 200 meters away, La Roche qui Pleure is a cliff where water constantly seeps through by filtration in the volcanic rock.

Rochester Falls is 4 kilometers from Souillac. What makes this waterfall unique are its perfectly geometric rectangular basalt columns – as if someone had stacked bricks of black rock. Swimming possible in the pool below. Take the path from the parking lot: 10 minutes on foot.

La Vanille Nature Park is home to Aldabra giant tortoises – some over 100 years old and 200 kilos. There are also Nile crocodiles and an insectarium. It's not a spectacular animal park, but the tortoises you can approach and touch are worth the trip, especially with children.

Bel Ombre is a former sugar estate converted in the southwest of the island, with preserved nature and hiking trails in the hills. Not essential, but if you pass through, the nature reserve adjoining the estate gives an idea of what Mauritius looked like before sugarcane.

The National History Museum in Mahébourg occupies an early-19th-century colonial house. It traces the Battle of Grand Port in 1810 – Napoleon's only naval victory against the British. The name "Grand Port" is engraved on the Arc de Triomphe in Paris, among the great battles of the Empire. Allow an hour. On Mondays, the Mahébourg market sets up in front: fruit, spices, crafts – arrive early.

Blue Bay is a protected marine park with one of the best-preserved coral reefs on the island. Snorkeling from the beach or glass-bottom boat excursion to see corals without getting wet. The water is shallow – 2 to 4 meters in places – making it one of the best snorkeling spots in Mauritius.
Pointe d'Esny is the beach next to Blue Bay, quieter, bordered by mangrove. A handful of small Creole houses, few people. If Blue Bay is crowded, retreat here.

Last morning swim at Trou aux Biches or Mont Choisy – the two finest beaches in the north, 10-15 minutes from Grand Baie. Trou aux Biches is livelier, Mont Choisy wilder. Choose according to your morning mood.

activities.text: Road day through the wild south. Take the coast, stop when you want – it's a day of mileage and discovery. Pack a picnic or eat at a small restaurant in Souillac: the village is calm, authentic, no tourists.

Mahébourg is an authentic fishing village with a waterfront where locals gather at day's end. No tourist commerce, no resort. A good evening to eat grilled fish at a small local restaurant and watch the sea.

Last hour of shopping before the airport: Chamarel rum, vanilla tea from Bois Chéri, market spices, scale models of traditional Mauritian boats. SSR Airport is about 1h15 via the highway from Grand Baie – don't leave too late.

days_plan.itin_title: Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast

visits.text: First day at Mont Choisy: adopt the beach of the same name, 3 km of white sand and casuarinas, one of the longest public beaches in the north. Flat lagoon, ideal swimming for children, regular sandy bottom. On weekends it's full of Mauritian picnics (improvised sega, curry pots), on weekdays you have the beach to yourself. Street vendors of ice cream and pineapple boy - pineapple peeled on the spot for 50 MUR, irresistible in the heat.

Cap Malheureux, 15 minutes north, and its famous Notre-Dame Auxiliatrice church with bright red roof built in 1938. Clear view of the northern islets (Coin de Mire, Île Plate, Île Ronde) that look set on the turquoise water. Few visitors during the week, end-of-the-world atmosphere. One of the most photographed spots on the island - and for once, it's deserved.

Grand Baie late afternoon, the main tourist village in the north. Lively waterfront, shops, ice cream parlors, henna tattoo artists for teenagers. Less tacky than they say, but clearly more commercial than the rest of the island. Those who hate noise will pass by; those who want a feet-in-the-sand dinner at sunset will be served (Le Capitaine, La Plage, La Sirène).

Catamaran day to the northern islets (Coin de Mire and Île Plate) or head southwest to Île aux Bénitiers - your choice depending on energy. Both leave from Grand Baie. Favor Ocean Cruises Mauritius (20 places maximum), far less crowded than the 60-80 person boats. On the program: sailing, two snorkeling stops, onboard barbecue lunch, and distant observation of dolphins in Tamarin Bay (from the boat only, no swimming). Count on 2,800 MUR adult, 1,400 MUR child.

Château de Labourdonnais at Mapou, neoclassical mansion from 1859 surrounded by a 540-hectare fruit estate. Visit with audio guide in French, walk through the orchards (mangoes, lychees, citrus), agricultural rum distillery on site. Children love the tasting at the end (fresh fruit juices, homemade jams). La Table du Château restaurant for lunch, formula at 850 MUR per adult, children's dish 350 MUR.

Eureka, the Creole house of Moka: 109 doors, 109 windows, it's the largest wooden colonial house still standing on the island, built in 1830. You visit the period-furnished rooms, lunch in the tropical garden with view of the ravine waterfall. Specificity: here they speak French-Creole with a drawling accent, they take their time. Funny anecdote for teens: the kitchen, 'slave quarters', is separated from the main house by a covered corridor to prevent smoke from invading the bedrooms.

Trou aux Cerfs at Curepipe, perfectly circular volcanic crater 350 meters in diameter and 85 meters deep, dormant for 700,000 years. Paved road that goes around: 1.2 km in 20 minutes, doable with stroller. 360° view over the central plateau and the Black River mountain range. Often rainy in Curepipe, bring a windbreaker even in July.

Quiet day to create a buffer before moving to Albion. Pereybère beach in the morning: small sheltered cove between Grand Baie and Cap Malheureux, white sand, crystal-clear water, and above all - less crowded than Mont Choisy. The reef is 20 meters from shore. Snack on site for lunch, no need to move.

Anse la Raie late afternoon: long beach facing Coin de Mire, ideal for kitesurfing in the austral winter (June-September). Guaranteed visual spectacle even without practicing: 30-40 colorful sails above the lagoon, you'd think you were at a competition. Kids love watching. Several schools give introductory courses from 12 years old, interesting for the teenager.

Île Plate (Flat Island) as an option: 3 km by speedboat, deserted beach, no infrastructure. Wilder than the usual islets. But access is complicated (only with licensed operator) and the crossing can be rough - avoid with a 6-year-old if the water is bad. Ask the operator the day before.

activities.text: Snorkeling at La Cuvette, small public beach hidden between two hotels in Grand Baie. Reef 30 meters from shore, clownfish in anemones, sometimes an octopus. Much quieter than nearby Trou aux Biches. Bring your gear, no rental here.

Evening aperitif on the Banana Beach Club rooftop or at the Beach House in Grand Baie. Nice mocktails for kids, tuna tartare for adults, view of the sunset over the bay. Coherent pricing (350 MUR cocktail, 800 MUR dish) for a place that could easily inflate prices.

Snorkeling at the Aquarium of Flic en Flac (one of the southwest catamaran stops): coral massif at 5 meters depth, nicknamed the aquarium for its density of angelfish, sergeant majors, parrotfish. Visibility over 15 meters in July. Float vest provided for children, masks and snorkels included in the outing price.

Break on the sand of Île aux Bénitiers or on Coin de Mire beach depending on the chosen option. Île aux Bénitiers: large beach with organized barbecue, sand mound in the middle of the water that emerges at low tide. Relaxed family atmosphere, very fine sand. Coin de Mire: wilder, landing sometimes complicated depending on swell.

Stop at the historic village of Mahébourg, fruit market and fresh spice stalls. Mauritian masala (unique blend with turmeric, coriander, cumin), vanilla pods at 100 MUR each, pickled vegetables in jars. Buy edible souvenirs without breaking the bank. Choose the merchant who lets you smell before buying - better sign of freshness.

Street food dinner at Trou aux Biches: vegetable or chicken curry roti at Chez Ram, takeaway to the beach for sunset. 60 MUR for stuffed roti, 30 MUR for alouda drink. More authentic and fresher than half the tourist restaurants in the area.

Local spa massage for parents, beach games for kids. Coco Spa in Grand Baie offers essential oil massages at 1,500 MUR per hour (3-4 times cheaper than in hotels) and watches children for 30 min in free play area. Very good reviews, book the day before.

Shopping at La Croisette in Grand Baie or at the Flic en Flac craft market: handmade boat models (the famous 'scale models' made by hand, from 800 MUR), embroidered cotton tablecloths, vanilla pods. Avoid the 'duty free' shops at Caudan, prices doubled.

days_plan.itin_title: Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne
Family Mauritius - 15 days around the island, from the southeast to Le Morne

visits.text: Get to the Central Market by 7am – that's when the stalls overflow with spices, tropical fruits, and fresh street food. Try the dholl puri, a split-pea-stuffed flatbread that's been sold on Port Louis streets for generations. Mine frit, gâteau piment, bol renversé: the market delivers the best meal of the day, and the cheapest.

Aapravasi Ghat has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2006. This wharf is where, starting in 1834, the first indentured laborers from India landed to replace enslaved workers after abolition. Between 1834 and 1924, nearly 500,000 people passed through. Plan for 45 minutes – the site is small but heavy with history.

The Blue Penny Museum holds two of the world's rarest stamps: the One Penny and Two Pence from 1847, the first stamps issued in Mauritius. They're only displayed in dim light to prevent deterioration. The museum also traces the island's history from the Portuguese onward – allow an hour.

Chinatown spreads around Rue Royale – a few blocks of pagodas, grocery stores, and Cantonese restaurants that recall Mauritius's waves of Chinese immigration since the 19th century. Not a polished tourist quarter: a genuine neighborhood enclave, with its smells and corner shops.

activities.text: Caudan Waterfront occupies renovated colonial warehouses facing the harbor. It's the most open-air corner of Port Louis – a solid option for a shaded lunch before the afternoon heat.

Climb up to Fort Adelaide – La Citadelle – late in the day. Built by the British between 1834 and 1840, it overlooks the city from 100 meters up. Late afternoon light on the harbor and Port Louis rooftops is the best you'll get all day. Bring water: the walk up from the center is steep.

days_plan.itin_title: Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast

visits.text: Île aux Cerfs from Trou d'Eau Douce, 10 minutes by water taxi. 87 hectares of white sand, swimming-pool-colored lagoon - if you go, do it early. Touristy, definitely really touristy, but genuinely beautiful: before 9am, the southeastern beaches are almost empty and it's sublime. After 11am, it's a parade of catamarans and the beach transforms into Marbella beach. Private water taxis negotiate at 350-400 MUR round trip without guide or barbecue, much cheaper than group excursions.

The Grande Rivière Sud-Est waterfall, accessible as an option from Île aux Cerfs or from Trou d'Eau Douce. 30 minutes by pirogue through the mangroves to the two falls about 30 meters high. The pirogue operators approach to within 5 meters of the water curtain, shower guaranteed. It's basic, but well placed in the program.

Domaine de l'Étoile, 1,200 hectares of private reserve 30 minutes from Trou d'Eau Douce. This is where you schedule the family buggy: minimum age 16 to drive, so dad or mom at the wheel and teen + little one as passengers in the back of a 4-seater buggy. 2-hour course through valleys, rivers and indigenous forests, stop in a river halfway to cool off. Probably the finest buggy spot on the island, and far less industrial than Casela. Count on 4,500 MUR for a 4-seater buggy.

Bras d'Eau National Park, a free and little-frequented alternative for a family forest walk. Marked trail of about 3 km around an old colonial reservoir, doable in 1h with children. Mauritius fodys (emblematic scarlet red bird) and kingfishers easily spotted. Information centers at the entrance lend maps and give daily tips.

Port Louis, the capital, 1h drive. Start with the Aapravasi Ghat, UNESCO-listed in 2006: this is where between 1834 and 1920 more than 450,000 Indian indentured laborers landed, the first great contract migration in history. Original staircases and buildings remain, free museum. Five minutes away, the central market explodes with colors and smells - go through the spice wing, Mauritian curry is half the price of Curepipe.

The Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam Botanical Garden at Pamplemousses, the oldest tropical garden in the southern hemisphere (1735). 37 hectares, more than 600 species including the famous giant Victoria amazonica water lilies 2 meters in diameter, strong enough to support a child - for the photo only, don't try. Avenue of talipot palms that only flower once in 60 years before dying. Guided tour recommended, 200 MUR for two hours with a French-speaking guide.

The Caudan Waterfront, old colonial port converted into a shopping-restaurant zone. Not revolutionary, but ideal for lunch after the market: shaded terraces, view of the sailboats, choice of cuisines (Creole, Indian, Chinese - Mauritius sums up its diversity in 100 square meters). The Crystal Pavilion serves a bol renversé at 350 MUR that fills a family.

activities.text: Snorkeling in the Île aux Cerfs lagoon on the east side, where the reef is most alive. Lots of butterflyfish, sergeant majors, sometimes stingrays in the sandy areas. The water is warm (24°C in July), shallow for 100 meters - suitable for the 6-year-old with a float vest. Water taxis provide fins-mask-snorkel on request, 100 MUR rental.

Beach barbecue lunch 'feet in the sand' on the island, either by booking via a water taxi (grilled fish + salad formula for 600 MUR/person), or bringing your own picnic. More authentic than catamaran buffets, which feel industrial and end up at 80 people per table.

Archery, horseback riding or zip-lining at Domaine de l'Étoile: the full range of outdoor activities on the island is concentrated here. Children from 6 years old can ride double with a guide on horseback (30-min pony ride, 800 MUR). The domain's advantage: you combine several activities on the same day with a single entrance ticket at 300 MUR/person, more economical than doing them separately elsewhere.

Sunset on Belle Mare beach, 15 minutes north. 8 km white sand beach, one of the island's longest, little frequented late in the day as hotels pull in their towels. Peaceful swimming, wind a bit stronger than at Trou d'Eau Douce. Small cocktail at the fishing village beach bar around 5:30pm.

Stop at the Champ de Mars, the oldest racecourse in the southern hemisphere (1812). If you happen on a race Saturday (May to November), free entry, electric atmosphere: 30,000 Mauritians in jeans and shirts yelling for their horse. Otherwise, view of the Citadel and skyline from the central lawn.

Snack at L'île Maurice - Le Vieux Conseil, a Creole café-pastry shop in the cobbled alley behind the town hall. Napolitaine (cake filled with guava jam), Mauritian mille-feuille, alouda (milk-vanilla-basil drink): the canonical sweet break for Mauritians on a shopping outing, at local prices (40 MUR for napolitaine).
